evidence_dialogues.utterance: Hey, how about changing the subject, Will?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: John

He elaborates that accumulating large properties like castles is pointless since his lifestyle demands that he is 'always leaving'.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: John

qa.question: When discussing the passage of time with Will, how many distinct chronological measurement units does John use to describe the turbulence of human lives?
option: A. None, he only uses the metaphor of a wheat field.

C. Two, mentioning only days and months.
D. Three, referring to a day, a year, or a century.
E. Four, referencing seconds, hours, days, and years.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: As one grows older, the days, weeks, months go by more quickly. What does a day or a year or a century mean to you? The birth-death cycle?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: John
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Turbulence. I meet someone, learn their name, say a word, they're gone. Others come like waves. Rise, fall. Ripples in a wheat field, blown by the wind.

reasoning_steps.inference: Will frames a question utilizing three specific units of time: a day, a year, or a century.
John accepts this premise and answers how those specific units of time (day, year, century) manifest to him as 'Turbulence'.

option: A. One.
B. Two.
C. Three.
D. Four.

evidence_dialogues.utterance: Edith, Dan and Sandy rise off the couch.

qa.question: What recurring conversational tactic does Will employ to challenge John's narrative about his extreme longevity?
option: A. He demands physical proof such as historical documents or photographs.
B. He violently threatens John's life at every possible opportunity.
C. He attempts to induce psychological guilt by emphasizing John's survival over his loved ones.
D. He constantly interrupts John with loud, dismissive laughter to undermine his credibility.
E. He ignores John entirely and focuses only on analyzing primitive history.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Are you comfortable knowing that you have lived while everyone you knew- Everyone you knew, John!- Has died?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Have you ever felt guilt about that- Something akin to survivor's guilt?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Bored, perhaps lonely, because your heart cannot keep its treasures. Is that what you're doing?

reasoning_steps.inference: Will repeatedly frames John's prolonged life as a source of inescapable emotional loss.
Will directly names and presses John to admit to 'survivor's guilt', showing a pattern of weaponizing John's supposed emotional trauma.

qa.question: What temporary emotional shift causes Will to abandon his armed interrogation of John?
option: A. He realizes John is telling the truth and becomes paralyzed with fear.
B. He is intimidated by Dan's physical threat and decides to back down.
C. He experiences a wave of weary resignation regarding his professional grading duties over the current conflict.
D. He suddenly remembers his wife's death and is overcome with debilitating grief.
E. He recognizes that John's healing abilities make shooting him pointless.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: (putting down the gun with a sigh)

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I have papers to correct. As much as I dislike that job, it'll be preferable to this. I leave you with it.

reasoning_steps.inference: Will physically de-escalates by putting the gun down and sighing.
He states his motivation for leaving is the mundane chore of grading papers, explicitly declaring that despite disliking the task, he finds it preferable to continuing the current situation.

qa.question: In Will's sequential escalation of accusations, what theory does he propose immediately after the suggestion that John unconsciously steals the life force of his peers?
option: A. That John deliberately murdered his past wives to keep his secret.
B. That John is an unknowing vampire filling graveyards with his companions.
C. That John invented the entire story to cover up a criminal past.
D. That John has led a wrongful life by abandoning his children.
E. That John is secretly suffering from severe survivor's guilt.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Perhaps you are! There have always been legends of such a thing, a creature, not quite human taking not the blood, but the life force itself?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Or are you a vampire, John? Even an unknowing one? Do you stand alive and tall in a graveyard that you helped to fill?

reasoning_steps.inference: Will first floats the idea of John taking the 'life force' of his peers.
Later in the sequence of accusations, Will graduates this theory to explicitly calling John a vampire, even an 'unknowing one'.

qa.question: What specific physical demonstration does Will propose to test the reality of John's biological claims?
option: A. Having John drink a lethal poison to observe his immunity.
B. Cutting John's hand with a knife to see how fast the tissue regenerates.
C. Inflicting a gunshot wound to the arm to watch the healing process.
D. Forcing John to hold his breath underwater for an extended period.
E. Exposing John to a severe infection to monitor his immune response.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: What do you think, John? A shot to the arm?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Perhaps we could watch it heal.

reasoning_steps.inference: Will suggests firing his weapon into John's arm.
The stated purpose of this violent act is strictly observational: to watch the subsequent healing process of the immortal body.

qa.question: What familial bond serves as the unstated catalyst for Will's intense focus on the cruelty of death and loss during his interrogation?
option: A. His relationship with his recently deceased father.
B. His impending divorce from his estranged wife.
C. His grief over the death of his wife, Mary.
D. His estrangement from his older brother.
E. His anxiety over his child's terminal illness.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: ...wife. Certainly you've had wives and children?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Harry
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Mary passed away yesterday.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Harry
evidence_dialogues.utterance: His wife. She had, uh, pancreatic cancer.

reasoning_steps.inference: Will displays extreme emotion when questioning John specifically about wives.
It is later explicitly confirmed by Harry that Mary was Will's wife and that she had died the day before.

qa.question: During his final threat, how many distinct bodily locations does Will specifically suggest targeting with his weapon?
option: A. One, just the chest.
B. Two, specifically the arm and the head.
C. Three, the arm, the leg, and the head.
D. None, he only threatens to shoot without specifying a location.
E. Four, referencing both arms and both legs.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: What do you think, John? A shot to the arm?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Will
evidence_dialogues.utterance: A bullet in the head- What exactly will happen?

reasoning_steps.inference: Will first proposes shooting John in the arm.
Will then escalates and proposes a second specific target location: the head.

session_1.text: Scene 9b: CharityNow and Death

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Come in.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: CHARITYNOW GUY #1 John Oldman?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Yes.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: CHARITYNOW GUY #1 CharityNow. We're here to pick up the furniture.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: It's all yours.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Here, take this chair. I'm gonna go drink in the corner.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: CharityNow guys start removing the furniture.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: You're, uh... you're donating it? Everything?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I'll get more.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Do you always travel this light?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: It's the only way to move.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Oh, you- You've talked a good deal about your extraordinary amount of living. What do you think of dying, John? Do you fear death?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Who wouldn't?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: How did primitive man regard death?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Well, we had the practical concept. You know, we stopped, fell down, didn't get up, started to smell bad, come apart.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Injuries we could understand- If someone's insides were all over the ground.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Infections... They were, uh, mysterious.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Aging... The biggest mystery of all.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: You realized you were different.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Uh, longer to realize how I was different, to find a way to synthesize my experience into a view of myself. At first, I thought everybody had something wrong with them. They got old and they died, animals, too... but not me.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: You live simply.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I've owned castles, but why leave a lot if you're always leaving? I have money.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: As one grows older, the days, weeks, months go by more quickly. What does a day or a year or a century mean to you? The birth-death cycle?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Turbulence. I meet someone, learn their name, say a word, they're gone. Others come like waves. Rise, fall. Ripples in a wheat field, blown by the wind.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Do you ever get tired of it all?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I get bored now and then. They keep making the same stupid mistakes over and over.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Hey! Then you see yourself as separate from the rest of humanity.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I didn't mean it that way. But of course...I am.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (Chuckling darkly)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Are you comfortable knowing that you have lived while everyone you knew- Everyone you knew, John!- Has died?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I've regretted losing people...often.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Have you ever felt guilt about that- Something akin to survivor's guilt?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: In the strict psychological sense? I suppose I have. Yeah. …But what can I do about it?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Indeed.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: CHARITYNOW GUY #2 (To Edith on the couch)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: I'm sorry, ma'am.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Edith, Dan and Sandy rise off the couch.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Gentlemen, I'm- I'm gonna keep the couch.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Thanks.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Ladies? Will?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Oh, no...

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: You've got a heart condition. Don't grump about it.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (scoffs, defeatedly)

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Hey, how about changing the subject, Will?

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Enough with the, uh- With the dying.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: But this is the flip side of this coin, Harry.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: I'm very curious to know his feelings. Would you prefer I asked him about his father?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I thought you always started with "tell me about your mother."

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Yes, but prehistory was strongly patriarchal.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Surely you remember your father?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I seem to remember a figure, perhaps an older brother, a social father, maybe.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Well, no matter. I can scarcely remember mine.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Do you feel a vacancy in your life about that, John, something you wish could be filled by a face, a voice, an image?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Not at this late date.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: There must be someone- Probably many- That you valued intensely?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (wearily)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Loved. You saw them age and die- A friend, a colleague, a- (hesitates, lump in throat)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: ...wife. Certainly you've had wives and children?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I'd move on. I had to move on.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Making him history's biggest bigamist.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: (Chuckles softly)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Have you ever in your life thought, "It should have been me"?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Maybe.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Yeah, Art has told me that some of your early fellows feared you were stealing their lives? Have you thought that perhaps you were?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Perhaps you are! There have always been legends of such a thing, a creature, not quite human taking not the blood, but the life force itself?

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: My god, Will.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (continuing)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: ...Unconsciously, perhaps, by some biological or psychic mechanism that we can only guess at? I'm not saying you would do such a thing deliberately. I'm not saying that you would even know how to...

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: ...Would you?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: ...But would such a thing be fair?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: So you believe me now?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: I'm only exploring what you have said. Whether I believe it or not is of no importance. We will die... You will live. Will you come to my funeral, John?

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Hey, Will...

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: You've gone too far. John didn't ask to be what he is.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: And we did not ask to hear about it. But if it were true, is there one among us who would not feel envy, even perhaps a touch of hatred? You told us of yourself, John. Can you imagine how we feel?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I never thought of that.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Since you may not die, while we most assuredly will... there must be a reason for that, no?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Perhaps you are an expert.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: CHARITYNOW GUY #1 Uh, that's it, Mr. Oldman. Have a good one.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Thank you. You too.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Music gets dark

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Or are you a vampire, John? Even an unknowing one? Do you stand alive and tall in a graveyard that you helped to fill?

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: That's going too far.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (dismissing Dan and continuing with gusto)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Bored, perhaps lonely, because your heart cannot keep its treasures. Is that what you're doing?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Have you led a wrongful life?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Well, then, perhaps...

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (reaching into coat pocket)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: …it is time to die.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (pulls out a gun)

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Wait a minute, now. Look, I don't know what John is doing, but I sure as hell don't like what you're doing.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Now you give me that gun or I'm gonna break your goddamn arm.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: (John reaches out an arm holding Dan back)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (scornfully)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: You sound like our football coach, Dan.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: What do you think, John? A shot to the arm?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Perhaps we could watch it heal.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: A bullet in the head- What exactly will happen?

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: Dramatic pause...

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (putting down the gun with a sigh)

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: I have papers to correct. As much as I dislike that job, it'll be preferable to this. I leave you with it.

session_1.speaker: Will
session_1.text: (storms off)

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Jesus Christ. What the hell was that all about?

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Where'd he get a gun?

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: He had you on the ropes, John. Are you really so damn smart?

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: It's not like Will.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: (quietly)

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Mary passed away yesterday.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: John glances up and dashes out of the door after Will.

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Who?

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: His wife. She had, uh, pancreatic cancer.
